title: React DevOps & Platform Engineering Mock Test
description: Advanced assessment for engineers focusing on the build, deployment, and infrastructure lifecycle of React applications.

questions.text: Which build-time optimization technique is used to split a large JavaScript bundle into smaller chunks that are loaded on demand?
options: Tree Shaking
Code Splitting
Minification
Brotli Compression
questions.explanation: Code splitting (often via React.lazy or Dynamic Imports) allows the browser to only download the code necessary for the current route, improving initial load time.

questions.text: In a Dockerized React environment, what is the best practice for serving the final production build?
options: Run `npm start` inside the container
Use a multi-stage build to compile the app, then serve static files using Nginx or Alpine
Install the full Node.js devDependencies in the production image
Mount the local source code folder as a volume
questions.explanation: Multi-stage builds allow you to keep the final image small by only including the production assets and a lightweight web server like Nginx.

questions.text: What is the primary difference between 'Static Site Generation' (SSG) and 'Server Side Rendering' (SSR) regarding infrastructure?
options: SSG requires a Node.js server at runtime; SSR does not
SSR requires a server to render pages on every request; SSG produces static files at build time
SSG is only for blogs; SSR is for dashboards
SSR is always faster for the global user base
questions.explanation: SSG can be served from a CDN (Edge), while SSR requires compute resources (Server/Lambda) to handle requests in real-time.

questions.text: How should 'Environment Variables' (e.g., API_URL) be handled for a React SPA that is built once but deployed to multiple environments (Dev/Staging/Prod)?
options: Hardcode them in the source code
Use a script to inject variables into the static build files at container runtime or use a config endpoint
Rebuild the entire application for every environment
Store them in the browser's localStorage manually
questions.explanation: Since React is compiled to static JS, variables are 'baked in' at build time. To 'build once, deploy anywhere,' you must use runtime injection or a configuration fetch.

questions.text: What is 'Tree Shaking' in the context of a Webpack or Vite build?
options: Deleting old files from the repository
Removing unused code (dead code) from the final bundle using ES6 module static analysis
Compressing images to reduce bundle size
Sorting components alphabetically
questions.explanation: Tree shaking relies on the static structure of ES6 modules to identify and exclude code that is never imported/used.

questions.text: Which HTTP header is most critical for ensuring that a React SPA's `index.html` is not cached indefinitely while its hashed assets (js/css) are?
options: Cache-Control: no-cache
Content-Type: text/html
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *
Strict-Transport-Security
questions.explanation: You want the browser to always check the server for a new index.html so it can pick up new hashed filenames, while the assets themselves can be cached long-term.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a 'Content Security Policy' (CSP) in a React application?
options: To speed up the rendering of components
To prevent Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) and data injection attacks by restricting which resources can be loaded
To manage user permissions and roles
To minify the HTML output
questions.explanation: CSP is a security layer that helps detect and mitigate certain types of attacks, including XSS and clickjacking.

questions.text: In a CI/CD pipeline for React, what is the 'Lockfile' (package-lock.json or yarn.lock) used for?
options: To encrypt the source code
To ensure that the exact same versions of dependencies are installed across all environments
To prevent other developers from editing the project
To store the API keys
questions.explanation: Lockfiles ensure deterministic builds, preventing 'it works on my machine' issues caused by minor version updates in dependencies.

questions.text: Which metric provides the best insight into how long a user is blocked from interacting with the page due to heavy JavaScript execution?
options: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint)
TBT (Total Blocking Time)
TTFB (Time to First Byte)
CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift)
questions.explanation: TBT measures the total amount of time between First Contentful Paint and Time to Interactive where the main thread was blocked.

options: Changing the color scheme of the app
Running two identical production environments and switching traffic from the old (Blue) to the new (Green)
Deploying the app to two different cloud providers
Testing the app in dark mode and light mode
questions.explanation: This strategy reduces downtime and risk by allowing you to verify the new version in a live-like environment before switching traffic.
